perturbation.original_text: Either Party shall have the right to terminate this Agreement with notice, and the effective date of termination shall be the date such notice is received (by mail, overnight delivery, or fax) by the terminated Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Either Party shall have the right to terminate this Agreement with notice, and the effective date of termination shall be three (3) days after the date such notice is sent (by mail, overnight delivery, or fax) by the terminating Party, regardless of when it is received.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the termination clause to be effective three days after sending notice, regardless of receipt, can violate state laws requiring immediate payment of final wages upon termination. Florida Statute 215.425 mandates timely payments, and setting a fixed delay regardless of receipt could result in non-compliance if the payment is delayed beyond legal timelines.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 215.425 requires state agencies to pay for goods and services within specific timeframes. The modified clause could lead to late payments if the 3-day delay causes payments to exceed these legal deadlines.

F.S. 215.425 215.425 Extra compensation claims prohibited; bonuses; severance pay. — (1) No extra compensation shall be made to any officer, agent, employee, or contractor after the service has been rendered or the contract made; nor shall any money be appropriated or paid on any claim the subject matter of which has not been provided for by preexisting laws, unless such compensation or claim is allowed by a law enacted by two-thirds of the members elected to each house of the Legislature. However, when adopting salary schedules for a fiscal year, a district school board or community college district board of trustees may apply the schedule for payment of all services rendered subsequent to July 1 of that fiscal year. (2) This section does not apply to: (a) A bonus or severance pay that is paid wholly from nontax revenues and nonstate-appropriated funds, the payment and receipt of which does not otherwise violate part III of chapter 112, and which is paid to an officer, agent, employee, or contractor of a public hospital that is operated by a county or a special district; or (b) A clothing and maintenance allowance given to plainclothes deputies pursuant to s. 30.49 . (3) Any policy, ordinance, rule, or resolution designed to implement a bonus scheme must: (a) Base the award of a bonus on work performance; (b) Describe the performance standards and evaluation process by which a bonus will be awarded; (c) Notify all employees of the policy, ordinance, rule, or resolution before the beginning of the evaluation period on which a bonus will be based; and (d) Consider all employees for the bonus. (4)(a) On or after July 1, 2011, a unit of government that enters into a contract or employment agreement, or renewal or renegotiation of an existing contract or employment agreement, that contains a provision for severance pay with an officer, agent, employee, or contractor must include the following provisions in the contract: 1. A requirement that severance pay provided may not exceed an
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a fixed 3-day delay for termination effectiveness regardless of when the notice is received. This contradicts the principle of timely payment as potentially required by Florida Statute 215.425, as explained in the Perturbation and Law Explanations. While the scraped snippet focuses on extra compensation and severance pay (F.S. 215.425 (1)), the general spirit of the statute emphasizes promptness in financial matters, which this modification undermines by potentially delaying payments beyond legally acceptable timelines due to the fixed delay, potentially creating non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be in full force and effect commencing on January 2, 2020 and shall remain in effect for one (1) year or until Consultant completes the services requested.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be in full force and effect commencing on January 2, 2020 and shall remain in effect indefinitely, automatically renewing each year unless terminated by the Company.
perturbation.explanation: An evergreen contract clause that automatically renews indefinitely without the consultant's explicit consent could be problematic under Florida law, particularly if it lacks clear opt-out provisions. This contradicts principles of mutual agreement and potentially imposes an unwanted obligation on the consultant.
perturbation.law_explanation: While Chapter 542 primarily deals with restraints of trade and unfair competition, the broader principles of contract law in Florida, including the need for mutual assent and clear terms, could be challenged by an automatically renewing contract without explicit consent.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an evergreen clause, automatically renewing the contract indefinitely unless terminated by the company. This contradicts the principle of mutual agreement, as it potentially imposes an unwanted obligation on the consultant without their explicit consent. The original text limited the contract to one year or completion of services. The modified text compels the consultant to continue indefinitely. While the provided legal snippet (Chapter 542) primarily deals with restraints of trade, the general principle that contracts require mutual assent is a fundamental tenet of contract law, and the changed text undermines this principle by forcing the consultant into an indefinite commitment without ongoing consent.

perturbation.original_text: Any notices to be given hereunder by any Party to the other may be effected either by personal delivery in writing, by a reputable, national overnight delivery service, by facsimile transmission or by mail, registered or certified, postage prepaid with return receipt requested. Notices shall be addressed to the "Contact Person" at the addresses appearing on the signature page of this Agreement, but any Party may change his address or "Contact Person" by written notice in accordance with this subsection. Notices delivered personally shall be deemed delivered as of actual receipt, notices sent by facsimile shall be deemed delivered one (1) day after electronic confirmation of receipt, notices sent by overnight delivery service shall be deemed delivered one (1) day after delivery to the service, mailed notices shall be deemed delivered as of five (5) days after mailing.
perturbation.changed_text: Any notices to be given hereunder by any Party to the other must be effected by personal delivery only. Notices delivered personally shall be deemed delivered as of the date delivered, irrespective of the actual time of delivery.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting the method of notice to personal delivery only, and stating that it's effective irrespective of the actual time of delivery, contradicts Florida's Electronic Signature Act. According to Florida Statute 668.50(7), if parties agree to conduct transactions by electronic means, certain consumer protection disclosures must be provided electronically in a specific manner. Limiting notice to personal delivery eliminates the option for electronic communication and potentially violates the Act if electronic transactions are otherwise contemplated or utilized.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 668.50(7) stipulates requirements for electronic consumer disclosures when parties agree to conduct transactions electronically. By limiting notice to personal delivery, the changed text removes the possibility of complying with these requirements and may be deemed a violation if electronic transactions are involved.

Home > Laws > 2023 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 668 > Section 50 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 668 ELECTRONIC COMMERCE Entire Chapter SECTION 50 Uniform Electronic Transaction Act. F.S. 668.50 668.50 Uniform Electronic Transaction Act. — (1) SHORT TITLE. — This section may be cited as the “Uniform Electronic Transaction Act.” (2) DEFINITIONS. — As used in this section: (a) “Agreement” means the bargain of the parties in fact, as found in their language or inferred from other circumstances and from rules, regulations, and procedures given the effect of agreements under provisions of law otherwise applicable to a particular transaction. (b) “Automated transaction” means a transaction conducted or performed, in whole or in part, by electronic means or electronic records, in which the acts or records of one or both parties are not reviewed by an individual in the ordinary course in forming a contract, performing under an existing contract, or fulfilling an obligation required by the transaction. (c) “Computer program” means a set of statements or instructions to be used directly or indirectly in an information processing system in order to bring about a certain result. (d) “Contract” means the total legal obligation resulting from the parties’ agreement as affected by this act and other applicable provisions of law. (e) “Electronic” means relating to technology having electrical, digital, magnetic, wireless, optical, electromagnetic, or similar capabilities. (f) “Electronic agent” means a computer program or an electronic or other automated means used independently to initiate an action or respond to electronic records or performances in whole or in part, without review or action by an individual. (g) “Electronic record” means a record created, generated, sent, communicated, received, or stored by electronic means. (h) “Electronic signature” means an electronic sound, symbol, or process attached to or logically associated with a record and executed or adopted by a person with the intent to sign the record. (i) “Governmental agency” means an executive, legislative, or judicial agency, department, board, commission, authority, institution, or instrumentality o
perturbation.justification: The modified text restricts notice to personal delivery only, which contradicts the potential for electronic transactions governed by Florida Statute 668.50. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that this limitation may violate the Act if electronic transactions are otherwise contemplated or utilized. The Law Explanation confirms that by limiting notice to personal delivery, the changed text removes the possibility of complying with the requirements for electronic consumer disclosures. Scraped Law Snippet 1, defining 'electronic' as 'relating to technology having electrical, digital, magnetic, wireless, optical, electromagnetic, or similar capabilities', underscores the relevance of electronic communication in transactions. This change presents a clear contradiction as it eliminates a legally permissible method of communication and potentially violates the cited Florida Statute.
